Wages, Prices, and Unemployment
We can capture our discussion of wage determination by using the following equation:
W = Pe F(u, z)
(7.1)
(−,+)
The aggregate nominal wage W depends on three factors:
■ The expected price level, Pe
■ The unemployment rate, u
■ A catch-all variable, z, that stands for all other variables that may affect the outcome
of wage setting.
Let’s look at each factor.
The Expected Price Level
First, ignore the difference between the expected and the actual price level and ask: Why
does the price level affect nominal wages? The answer: Because both workers and firms
care about real wages, not nominal wages.
■ Workers do not care about how many dollars they receive but about how many
goods they can buy with those dollars. In other words, they care about the nominal
wages (W) they receive relative to the price of the goods they buy (P). They care
about W P.
■ In the same way, firms do not care about the nominal wages they pay but about the
nominal wages (W) they pay relative to the price of the goods they sell (P). So they
also care about W P.
Think of it another way. If workers expect the price level—the price of the goods they
buy—to double, they will ask for a doubling of their nominal wage. If firms expect the
price level—the price of the goods they sell—to double, they will be willing to double the
nominal wage. So, if both workers and firms expect the price level to double, they will
agree to double the nominal wage, keeping the real wage constant. This is captured in
Chapter 7 The Labor Market 143

--- PAGE 165 ---

An increase in the expected equation (7.1): A doubling in the expected price level leads to a doubling of the nominal
price level leads to an c wage chosen when wages are set.
increase in the nominal wage
Why do wages depend on the expected price level, Pe, rather than the actual price level, P?
in the same proportion.
Because wages are set in nominal (dollar) terms, and when they are set, the relevant
price level is not yet known.
For example, in some union contracts in the United States, nominal wages are set
in advance for three years. Unions and firms have to decide what nominal wages will be
over the following three years based on what they expect the price level to be over those
three years. Even when wages are set by firms, or by bargaining between the firm and
each worker, nominal wages are typically set for a year. If the price level goes up unex-
pectedly during the year, nominal wages are typically not readjusted.(How workers and
firms form expectations of the price level will occupy us for much of the next two chap-
ters; we will leave this issue aside for the moment.)
The Unemployment Rate
Also affecting the wage in equation (7.1) is the unemployment rate, u. The minus sign
under u indicates that an increase in the unemployment rate decreases wages.
The fact that wages depend on the unemployment rate was one of the main conclusions
of our previous discussion. If we think of wages as being determined by bargaining, then
higher unemployment weakens workers’ bargaining power, forcing them to accept lower
An increase in unemployment
wages. If we think of wages as being determined by efficiency wage considerations, then
leads to a decrease in the
nominal wage.c higher unemployment allows firms to pay lower wages and still keep workers willing to work.
The unemployment rate is not the only measure and may not even be the best mea-
sure of the state of the labor market. Another measure is the ratio of vacancies to unem-
ployment: More vacancies mean more firms looking for workers; more unemployment
means more workers looking for jobs. The more vacancies, the less unemployment, the
stronger workers are in bargaining. I shall use unemployment here.
The Other Factors
The third variable in equation (7.1), z, is a catch-all variable that stands for all the factors
that affect wages given the expected price level and the unemployment rate. By conven-
tion, we will define z so that an increase in z implies an increase in the wage (thus, the
By the definition of z, an
positive sign under z in the equation). Our previous discussion suggests a long list of
increase in z leads to an
increase in the nominal wage.c potential factors here.
Take, for example, unemployment insurance, the payment of unemployment
benefits to workers who lose their jobs. There are good reasons why society should pro-
vide some insurance to workers who lose their job and have a hard time finding another.
But there is little question that, by making the prospects of unemployment less distress-
ing, more generous unemployment benefits do increase wages at a given unemploy-
ment rate. If unemployment insurance did not exist, some workers would have little
to live on and would be willing to accept low wages to avoid remaining unemployed.
But—fortunately—unemployment insurance does exist, and it allows unemployed
workers to hold out for higher wages. In this case, we can think of z as representing the
level of unemployment benefits. At a given unemployment rate, higher unemployment
benefits increase the wage.
It is easy to think of other factors. An increase in the minimum wage may also i ncrease
wages just above the minimum wage, leading to an increase in the average wage, W,
at a given unemployment rate. Or take an increase in employment protection, which
makes it more expensive for firms to lay off workers. Such a change is likely to increase the
bargaining power of workers covered by this protection (laying them off and hiring other
workers is now costlier for firms), increasing the wage for a given unemployment rate.
144 The Medium Run The Core

--- PAGE 166 ---

The list does not stop there. Globalization, by increasing competition with other
countries, also affect wages. Demographics play a role. Young workers change jobs
more often than older workers. An aging society has less mobile workers. Companies
like Uber may offer more flexible jobs, but lower pay. The internet facilitates the
search for workers by firms, and the search for jobs by workers. The list is long, and
the effects on the natural rate often complex. We will explore some of these factors as
we go along.
7-4 PRICE DETERMINATION
Having looked at wage determination, let’s now turn to price determination.
The prices set by firms depend on the costs they face. These costs depend, in turn, on
the nature of the production function, which is the relation between the inputs used
in production and the quantity of output produced, and on the prices of these inputs.
For the moment, we will assume that firms produce goods using labor as the only Using a term from micro-
factor of production. We will write the production function as follows: economics, this assumption
implies constant returns to
Y = AN labor in production. If firms
double the number of work-
where Y is output, A is labor productivity, and N is employment. This way of writing the
ers they employ, they double
production function implies that labor productivity, which is defined as output per the amount of output they
worker, is constant. b produce.
It should be clear that this is a strong simplification. In reality, firms use other factors
of production in addition to labor. They use capital—machines and factories. They use
raw materials—oil, for example. Moreover, there is technological progress, so that labor
productivity (A) is not constant but steadily increases over time. We shall introduce
these complications later. We shall introduce other inputs in Chapter 8 when we discuss
changes in the price of oil, and we shall see how this explains part of the Covid inflation
we discussed in Chapter 1. We shall focus on the role of capital and technological prog-
ress when we turn to the determination of output in the long run in Chapter 10 through
Chapter 13. For the moment, though, this simple relation between output and employ-
ment will serve our purposes.
Given the assumption that labor productivity, A, is constant, we can make one fur-
ther simplification. We can choose the units of output so that one worker produces one
unit of output—in other words, so that A = 1. (This way we do not have to carry the
letter A around, and this will simplify notation.) With this assumption, the production
function becomes:
Y = N (7.2)
The production function Y = N implies that the cost of producing one more unit
of output is the cost of employing one more worker, at wage W. Using the terminology
introduced in your microeconomics course: The marginal cost of production—the cost
of producing one more unit of output—is equal to W.
If there was perfect competition in the goods market, the price of a unit of output
would be equal to marginal cost: P would be equal to W. But many goods markets are not
competitive, and firms charge a price higher than their marginal cost. A simple way of
capturing this fact is to assume that firms set their price according to
P = (1+m) W (7.3)
where m is the markup of the price over the cost. To the extent that goods markets are
not competitive and firms have market power, m is positive, and the price, P, will exceed
the cost, W, by a factor equal to (1+m).

7-5 THE NATURAL RATE
OF UNEMPLOYMENT
Let’s now look at the implications of wage and price determination for unemployment.
For the rest of this chapter, we shall do so under the assumption that nominal wages
depend on the actual price level, P, rather than on the expected price level, Pe (why we
make this assumption will become clear soon). Under this additional assumption, wage

based on the assumption
that Pe = P.c the natural rate of unemployment. Let’s see how.
The Wage-Setting Relation
Given the assumption that nominal wages depend on the actual price level (P) rather
than on the expected price level (Pe), equation (7.1), which characterizes wage determi-
nation, becomes:
W = P F(u, z)

The Price-Setting Relation
Let’s now look at the implications of price determination. If we divide both sides of the
price determination equation, (7.3), by the nominal wage, we get
P
= 1+m (7.5)
W
The ratio of the price level to the wage implied by the price-setting behavior of firms
equals 1 plus the markup. Now invert both sides of this equation to get the implied real wage:
W 1
= (7.6)
P 1+m
Note what this equation says: Price-setting decisions determine the real wage paid by firms.
An increase in the markup leads firms to increase their prices given the wage they have
to pay; equivalently, it leads to a decrease in the real wage.
The step from equation (7.5) to equation (7.6) is algebraically straightforward. But
how price setting actually determines the real wage paid by firms may not be intuitively
obvious. Think of it this way: Suppose the firm you work for increases its markup and
therefore increases the price of its product. Your real wage does not change much; you are
still paid the same nominal wage, and the product produced by the firm is likely a small part
of your consumption basket. Now suppose that not only the firm you work for but all the
firms in the economy increase their markup. All the prices go up. Even if you are paid
the same nominal wage, your real wage goes down. So, the higher the markup set by firms, the
lower your (and everyone else’s) real wage will be. This is what equation (7.6) says.
The price-setting relation in equation (7.6) is drawn as the horizontal line PS (for
price setting) in Figure 7-6. The real wage implied by price setting is 1(1+m); it does
not depend on the unemployment rate.

equal to the real wage implied by price setting. (This way of stating equilibrium may
sound strange if you learned to think in terms of labor supply and labor demand in your

Factors like the generosity of unemployment benefits or antitrust legislation can hardly be
thought of as the result of nature. Rather, they reflect various characteristics of the structure
of the economy. For that reason, a better name for the equilibrium rate of unemployment
would be the structural rate of unemployment, but so far the name has not caught on.
Because unemployment rates vary cyclically, natural unemployment rates are
not directly observable. But, if we look over sufficient long periods of time, the average
unemployment rate should be a good proxy for the natural rate. And, as is shown in the
Focus box above, “What Explains European Unemployment?”, there are large differences
in average unemployment rates across countries. Identifying the sources of these differ-
ences is clearly an important policy question.
7-6 WHERE WE GO FROM HERE
We have just seen how equilibrium in the labor market determines the equilibrium
unemployment rate (called the natural rate of unemployment). Although we leave a precise
derivation to Chapter 9, it is clear that, for a given labor force, the unemployment rate

--- PAGE 172 ---

determines the level of employment, and that, given the production function, the level
of employment determines the level of output. Thus, associated with the natural rate of
unemployment is a natural level of output.
So, you may (and, indeed, you should) ask: What did we do in the previous four
chapters? If equilibrium in the labor market determines the unemployment rate and,
by implication, the level of output, why did we spend so much time looking at the goods
and financial markets? What about our previous conclusions that the level of output
was determined by factors such as monetary policy, fiscal policy, consumer confidence,
and so on—all factors that do not enter equation (7.7) and therefore do not affect the
natural level of output?
The key to the answer lies in the difference between the short run and the medium run:
■ We have derived the natural rate of unemployment and, by implication, the associ-
ated level of output under two assumptions. First, we have assumed equilibrium
in the labor market. Second, we have assumed that the price level was equal to the
expected price level.
■ However, there is no reason for the second assumption to be true in the short run.
The price level may well turn out to be different from what was expected when nomi-
nal wages were set. Hence, in the short run, there is no reason for unemployment to In the short run, the factors
be equal to the natural rate or for output to be equal to its natural level. that determine movements
in output are the factors we
As we shall see in Chapter 9, the factors that determine movements in output
focused on in the preced-
in the short run are indeed the factors we focused on in the preceding three chapters:
ing four chapters: monetary
monetary policy, fiscal policy, and so on. Your time (and mine) was not wasted. b policy, fiscal policy, and so on.
■ But expectations are unlikely to be systematically wrong (say, too high or too low)
forever. That is why, in the medium run, output tends to return to its natural level.
In the medium run, the factors that determine unemployment and output are the fac-
In the medium run, output
tors that appear in equation (7.7). b
tends to return to the natural
These, in short, are the answers to the questions asked in the first paragraph of this level. The factors that deter-
mine unemployment and, by
chapter. Developing these answers in detail will be our task in the next two chapters.
implication, output are the
Chapter 8 relaxes the assumption that the price level is equal to the expected price level
factors we have focused on
and derives the relation between unemployment and inflation known as the Phillips this chapter.
curve. Chapter 9 puts all the pieces together.

macroeconomic policy became a discussion about which point to choose on the Phillips curve.

OECD countries, there was both high inflation and high unemployment, clearly contradicting
the original Phillips curve. A relation reappeared, but as a relation between the change in the There are regular announce-

If you remember a basic message from this chapter, it should be: Low unemploy-
ment puts upward pressure on inflation, but the form of the relation depends very
much on how people and firms form expectations.
8-1 INFLATION, EXPECTED INFLATION,
AND UNEMPLOYMENT
In Chapter 7, we derived the following equation for wage determination (equation (7.1)):
W = Pe F(u, z)
The nominal wage (W), set by wage setters, depends on the expected price level
(Pe), the unemployment rate (u), and a variable (z) that captures all the other factors
that affect wage determination, from unemployment benefits to the form of collective
bargaining.
It will be convenient to assume a specific form for the function (F):
F (u, z) = 1−αu+ z
This captures the notion that the higher the unemployment rate, the lower the
nominal wage; and the higher z (for example, the more generous the unemployment
benefits), the higher the nominal wage. The parameter α (the lowercase Greek letter
alpha) captures the strength of the effect of unemployment on the wage. Replacing the
function (F) by this specific form in the equation above gives
W = Pe(1−αu+ z)

--- PAGE 180 ---

Also in Chapter 7, we derived the following equation for price determination (equa-
tion (7.3)):
P = (1+m)W
The price (P) set by firms (equivalently, the price level) is equal to the nominal wage (W)
times 1 plus the markup of prices over wages (m).
We then used these two relations together with the additional assumption that the
actual price level was equal to the expected price level. Under this additional assumption,
we then derived the natural rate of unemployment. We now explore what happens when
we do not impose this additional assumption.
Replacing the nominal wage in the second equation by its expression from the first gives
P = Pe (1+m)(1−αu+ z) (8.1)
This gives us a relation between the price level (P), the expected price level (Pe), and the
unemployment rate (u). From now on, to lighten my
Let π denote the inflation rate and πe the expected inflation rate. Then equation (8.1) writing, I shall often refer to
the inflation rate simply as
can be rewritten as a relation between inflation, expected inflation, and the unemployment rate:b

Deriving equation (8.2) from equation (8.1) is not difficult, but it is tedious, so it is
left to an appendix at the end of this chapter. It is important that you understand each of
the effects at work: b Equation (8.2) is one of the
most important equations in
■ An increase in expected inflation (πe) leads to an increase in actual inflation (π). macroeconomics
To see why, start from equation (8.1). An increase in the expected price level (Pe) leads,
one for one, to an increase in the actual price level (P): If wage setters expect a higher
price level, they set a higher nominal wage, which leads to an increase in the price level.
Now note that, given last period’s price level, a higher price level this period
implies a higher rate of increase in the price level from last period to this period—that
is, higher inflation. Similarly, given last period’s price level, a higher expected price
level this period implies a higher expected rate of increase in the price level from last
period to this period—that is, higher expected inflation. Thus the fact that an increase
in the expected price level leads to an increase in the actual price level can be restated
as: An increase in expected inflation leads to an increase in actual inflation. b Increase in πe⇒
■ Given expected inflation (πe) and the unemployment rate (u), an increase in the markup Increase in π.
(m) or in the factors that affect wage determination—an increase in z—leads to an
increase in actual inflation (π).

m or z increases the price level (P). Using the same argument as in the previous
bullet to restate this proposition in terms of inflation and expected inflation: Given
expected inflation (πe), an increase in either m or z leads to higher inflation (π). b Increase in m or z⇒
■ Given expected inflation (πe), a decrease in the unemployment rate (u) leads to an increase Increase in π.
in actual inflation (π).

(P). Restating this in terms of inflation and expected inflation: Given expected infla-
tion (πe), an increase in the unemployment rate (u) leads to higher inflation (π). b Decrease in u⇒
Increase in π.

8-2 THE ROLE OF INFLATION
EXPECTATIONS
Let’s start with the relation between unemployment and inflation as it was first discov-
ered by Phillips, Samuelson, and Solow.
The Original Phillips Curve
Assume that inflation varies from year to year around some value π and that it is not
persistent, so that inflation this year is not helpful in predicting inflation next year. This
happens to be a good characterization of the behavior of inflation over the periods that
Phillips or Solow and Samuelson studied. In such an environment, it makes sense for
wage setters to expect that, whatever inflation was last year, inflation this year will be
equal to π, so πe = π and equation (8.5) becomes
t
π = π −α(u −u ) (8.6)
t t n
In this case, we should observe a negative relation between unemployment and
inflation. This is precisely the negative relation between unemployment and inflation that
Phillips found for the United Kingdom and Solow and Samuelson found for the United
States. When unemployment was high, inflation was low, even sometimes negative.
When unemployment was low, inflation was positive.
160 The Medium Run The Core

--- PAGE 182 ---

When these findings were published, they suggested that policymakers faced a
trade-off between inflation and unemployment: If they were willing to accept more infla-
tion, they could achieve lower unemployment. This looked like an attractive trade-off,
and starting in the early 1960s, US macroeconomic policy aimed at steadily decreasing
unemployment. Figure 8-2 plots the combinations of the inflation rate and the unem-
ployment rate in the United States for each year from 1961 to 1969. Note how well the
negative relation between unemployment and inflation, corresponding to equation (8.4),
held during the long economic expansion through most of the 1960s. From 1961 to
1969, the unemployment rate declined steadily, from 6.8% to 3.4%, and the inflation rate
steadily increased, from 1.0% to 5.5%. Put informally, the US economy moved up along
the Phillips curve. It indeed appeared that, if policymakers were willing to accept higher
inflation, they could achieve and maintain lower unemployment.
Around 1970, however, the relation between the inflation rate and the unemploy-
ment rate, so clear in Figure 8-2, broke down. Figure 8-3 shows the combination of
the inflation and the unemployment rate in the United States for each year from 1970
to 1995. The points are scattered. There was no longer any visible relation between the
unemployment rate and the inflation rate.
Why did the original Phillips curve vanish? Because wage setters changed the way they
formed their expectations about inflation.
This change came from the change in the behavior of inflation. As policymakers
were willing to tolerate higher inflation in order to reduce unemployment, the rate of
inflation became more persistent. High inflation in one year became more likely to be
followed by high inflation the next year. As a result, people, when forming expectations,
started to take into account the persistence of inflation, and this change in expectation
formation changed the nature of the relation between unemployment and inflation. In
macroeconomic jargon, expectations that had been anchored (i.e., roughly constant)
became increasingly deanchored.
Let’s look at the argument in the previous paragraph more closely. Suppose expecta-
tions of inflation are formed according to
πe = (1−θ)π +θπ (8.7)
t t−1
In words: Expected inflation this year depends partly on a constant value, π, with
weight 1−θ, and partly on inflation last year, which we denote by π , with weight θ. The

higher the value of θ, the more last year’s inflation leads workers and firms to revise their
expectations of what inflation will be this year, and so the higher the expected inflation rate.
We can then think of what happened in the 1970s as an increase in the value of θ
over time:
■ So long as inflation was stable, it was reasonable for workers and firms to just ignore
past inflation and to assume a constant value for inflation. For the periods that
Phillips and Samuelson and Solow looked at, θ was close to zero and expectations
were roughly given by πe = π. The Phillips curve was given by equation (8.6).
■ But as inflation started to increase, workers and firms started changing the way they
formed expectations. They started assuming that, if inflation was high last year, it
was likely to be high this year as well. The parameter θ, the effect of last year’s infla-
tion rate on this year’s expected inflation rate, increased. By the mid-1970s, people
expected this year’s inflation rate to be the same as last year’s inflation rate—in
other words, that θ was now equal to 1.
Now turn to the implications of different values of θ for the relation between infla-
tion and unemployment. Substitute equation (8.7) for the value of πe in equation (8.5):

He went on:
To state [my] conclusion differently, there is always a tem-
porary trade-off between inflation and unemployment;
So, when θ equals1, the unemployment rate affects not the inflation rate but rather
the change in the inflation rate. High unemployment leads to decreasing inflation; low
unemployment leads to increasing inflation.
This discussion is the key to what happened after 1970. As θ increased from 0 to 1,
the simple relation between the unemployment rate and the inflation rate disappeared.
This disappearance is what we saw in Figure 8-3. By the mid-1970s, the Phillips curve
relation became a relation between the change in the rate of inflation and the difference
between the unemployment rate and the natural rate. To distinguish it from the original
Phillips curve, it became known as the accelerationist Phillips curve (to indicate that
a low unemployment rate leads to an increase in the inflation rate and thus an accelera-
tion of the price level). b Original Phillips curve:
This however was not the end of the story, and the relation changed again, starting Low u ⇒ High inflation.
t
in the late 1990s, as shown in Figure 8-4. The figure plots the value of θ obtained by Accelerationist Phillips curve:
estimating equation (8.8) over time. Each point corresponds to the result of estimation Low u ⇒ Increasing inflation.
t
using data from the previous 20 years, so, for example the point for 1972 shows the
estimated value of θ based on estimation using data from 1953 to 1972. The two bands
around the estimated value give the 90% confidence interval around the estimate: They
tell us, for each 20-year sample, that there is 90% probability that the true value of θ is
between the upper and the lower value of the band.
You can see the initial increase of θ from 1966 on, which we just discussed, and how
θ remained close to 1 until the late 1990s. From the late1990s on, however, θ started to
decrease, and by 2020 it was again very close to zero. (You should ignore the fact that
the estimated values of θ at the end are actually negative. This is unlikely, and, as shown
by the confidence bands, reflects the fact that we do not know the exact value of θ.)

In other words, by 2020 or so, the relation between unemployment and inflation
was again better captured by the original Phillips curve than by the accelerationist ver-
sion. What explains it?
The answer: Increased credibility of the Fed.
Starting in the early 1980s, the Fed emphasized its commitment to maintaining low
and stable inflation. In 2012, it adopted an explicit target inflation of 2%. By the late
1990s, it had largely achieved its goal. Inflation had been stable for more than a decade,
and so the way people formed expectations adjusted again. Even if inflation was higher
than the target in a given year, people assumed that the central bank would take mea-
sures to return inflation to its target value; expected inflation became roughly constant,
roughly equal to the target inflation set by the Fed. Expectations of inflation that had
become de-anchored during the 1970s and the 1980s became re-anchored, reacting little
or not at all to movements in actual inflation.
The 2020s have introduced yet another twist. Go back to Figure 2-6, which plotted
the quarterly unemployment rate and the quarterly inflation rate since 2000. While,
from 2000 to 2020, there was indeed a stable negative relation between the inflation
rate and the unemployment rate, inflation in 2021 and 2022 (the eight quarterly dots
much above the line) was much higher than would have been predicted on the basis of
this relation, represented by the regression line in the figure. What happened, and what
it implies, is the topic of the next section.
8-3 THE ROLE OF SUPPLY SHOCKS, AND
COVID INFLATION
The previous section assumed that the three parameters m, z, and α, were constant,
which implied that the natural rate of unemployment (u ), which is equal to (m+ z) α,
n
was also constant.
This is unlikely to be the case: As we discussed in Chapter 7, these parameters reflect
a large number of factors—from labor regulations affecting wage determination to
monopoly power affecting price setting—which are likely to evolve over time. Most of the
time these changes are slow, so assuming that u is constant is an acceptable approxima-
n
tion. Indeed, if you look at the fluctuations of the unemployment rate in the United States

in Figure 2-3, it looks like the rate varies around a fairly stable value, which we can think
of as the natural unemployment rate, and does not seem very different today than it was
in the 1960s.
Once in a while, however, there are major movements in m, typically due to an
increase in the cost of nonlabor inputs. This was for example the case in the 1970s, when
the formation of OPEC (the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries), a cartel of
oil producers that was able to act as a monopolist, led to a fivefold increase in the price of
oil. It was also the case during the Covid crisis, with a major increase in the price of com-
modities. When the cost of nonlabor inputs increases, firms pass on the higher costs in
prices, leading to an increase in the price level given wages, thus leading to an increase
in m.
To see what this implies, go back to equation (8.4). The increase in m, given z and α, b Recall: P = (1+ m) W⇒
leads to an increase in the natural unemployment rate. We discussed why in Chapter 7: W P = 1(1+ m)
An increase in m, which is an increase in the price level given the nominal wage, implies
mechanically a decrease in the real wage, the ratio of the nominal wage to the price level.
What happens to inflation then depends on two factors. It may be that that workers
accept the reduction in real wages at the same unemployment rate; in terms of equation
(8.4), z decreases, offsetting the increase in m. Or it may be that firms decide to absorb the
cost increase through a decrease in profit margins, so that m does not increase. Unless these
assumptions are fully satisfied, which is unlikely, (m+ z) goes up, leading to an increase in
the natural unemployment rate.
Assume therefore that (m+ z) goes up, at least for some time. We can express what
happens to inflation in one of two equivalent ways. Let’s go back to equation (8.3):
π = πe +(m+ z)−αu
t t t
For a given unemployment rate (u ) and expected inflation (πe), inflation goes up,
t t
reflecting the increase in (m+ z).
Or, reproducing equation (8.5):
π −πe = −α(u −u )
t t t n
For a given unemployment rate (u ) and expected inflation (πe), the natural unemploy-
t t
ment rate goes up, leading to an increase in inflation.
Suppose that, before the increase in (m+ z), the unemployment rate was equal to
the natural rate, so inflation was equal to expected inflation. Suppose that actual unem-
ployment remains unchanged, so it is now lower than the natural rate. What happens
over time to inflation then depends on two factors, the persistence of the shocks and the
adjustment of expectations.
■ First, and obviously, it depends on whether and how fast (m+ z) returns to its ini-
tial value. So long as it remains higher than before, inflation remains higher than
expected inflation.
■ Second, what this implies for inflation depends on what we discussed in the previ-
ous section, namely the adjustment of expectations. If expectations remain fully
anchored, so πe = π, then, when (m+ z) is back to its previous value, inflation
t
returns to target—there is a burst of inflation, but it goes away on its own. When
(m+ z) is back to its initial value, inflation is again equal to expected inflation, the
unemployment rate is equal to the natural rate, and there is no need for monetary
policy to increase unemployment.
This however is a best-case scenario. Suppose that inflation lasts for a while and
leads wage setters to question the commitment of the central bank to achieve its target,
so they start adjusting their inflation expectations to reflect actual inflation. In terms of
the equations in the previous section, θ increases.
Chapter 8 The Phillips Curve, the Natural Rate of Unemployment, and Inflation 165

--- PAGE 187 ---

For simplicity, take the extreme case where θ increases all the way to equal 1, so
πe = π and the behavior of inflation is given by π −π = −αu +(m+ z). Then,
t t−1 t t−1 t
so long as (m+ z) exceeds its previous value, inflation keeps increasing. Even when
(m+ z) is back to its previous value, inflation, which has increased along the way, is
now higher than it was before the shock, and a period of higher unemployment is needed
to return it to target. This is a much less attractive scenario and has important implica-
tions for the conduct of monetary policy, to which we shall return in Chapter 9.
The roles of supply shocks, overheating, and expectation formation are well illus-
trated by the burst of inflation associated with the Covid crisis.
The start of the Covid crisis in the first quarter of 2020 was associated, in the
United States, with lockdowns, a large increase in the unemployment rate, and an initial
decrease in the inflation rate. The quarterly inflation rate, based on the CPI measure of
the price level and measured at annual rates, went briefly negative. As the blue line in
Figure 8-5 shows, however, from then on inflation increased, exceeding 10% in mid-
2022, far above the Fed’s 2% target inflation. It started decreasing in the third quarter of
2022 and, in the fourth quarter, was down to about 3%.
What triggered the burst of inflation?
On the demand side, two factors clearly played a role. The first was pent-up
demand from consumers, who had been unwilling (because of the uncertainty) and often
unable (because of the lockdowns) to spend. The second was a series of generous fis-
cal programs, aimed at helping households survive high unemployment and the loss of
income as well as helping firms survive the decrease in sales due to lockdowns. A $2 trillion
package in March 2020 by the Trump administration was followed by $0.9 trillion in
December 2020, and another $1.9 trillion by the Biden administration in March 2021;
together these represented an unprecedented peacetime fiscal expansion, equal to 15%
of 2020 GDP.
By March 2021, it was clear that these packages were going to have a large effect
on the demand for goods and services. There were two diverging views as to what would
happen to inflation.
■ A majority view, including the view held by the Fed, was that while the increase in
demand might be very large and lead to a large decrease in unemployment, the effect on
inflation was likely to be limited. In terms of equation (8.5), the argument was that α
was small, expectations were well anchored, and θ would remain small as well.

■ A minority view was that, given the size of the likely increase in demand, the
relations that had held well over the previous two decades could not be taken for
granted. Unemployment might become so low as to lead to much more inflation
pressure and α might become much larger. And with higher inflation, expectations
might start deanchoring and θ might increase. Both effects might combine to trigger
high inflation.
It turned out that both sides were partly right and partly wrong. There was indeed
substantial inflation, so the optimists were wrong, but it did not come mainly from the
effect of unemployment nor from the deanchoring of expectations, so the pessimists
were wrong about the mechanism that led to high inflation.
The main channel turned out to be an increase in prices given wages, or, in terms of
equation (8.5), an increase in the markup (m). This was for two reasons.
■ First, the world demand for commodities, from food to raw materials to oil, went up,
partly because of high US demand, partly from higher demand worldwide as econo-
mies recovered from their initial drop. From the first quarter of 2021, the price of
energy in particular increased substantially, as shown by the red line in Figure 8-5,
which plots the rate of change of the price of the energy component of the CPI.
Then in the first quarter of 2022, the start of the Ukraine war and the embargo on
Russian oil led to further increases, until the price turned around in the second half
of 2022. The higher cost of energy increased the cost of production for firms, lead-
ing them to increase their prices, given wages.
■ Second, supply chains were disrupted. Even when lockdowns were lifted, they had
lasting effects on supply chains, both international and national. Critical interme-
diate inputs were not available, leading to shortages and higher prices. For e xample,
shortages of semiconductor chips had major effects on automobile production,
leading to stockouts and much higher prices not only on new cars but also on used
cars as consumers turned to them when they were unable to buy new cars.
Figure 8-6 shows one measure of shortages that captures well what happened: the
number of Google Trends queries for the word “shortage” in the United States. The mea-
sure peaked in the second quarter of 2021 as the demand for goods, fueled by the March
fiscal program, increased in the face of substantial limits on production. Although
the measure had decreased at the time of writing (fourth quarter of 2022), it remains

higher than pre-Covid. Covid is not the only culprit; tensions between the United States,
Russia, and China have also disrupted supply chains.
This was not the first time that the US economy was hit by large increases in energy
prices. As mentioned earlier, the 1970s saw a fivefold increase in the price of oil. The
direct effect was to increase inflation. The indirect effect, however, was also to de-anchor
inflation expectations, increasing θ and leading to even higher inflation: Inflation
reached 17% at an annual rate in the first quarter of 1980 and it took nearly four years
and a period of high unemployment to get it down to 4%.
The good news, this time, is that inflation expectations did not substantially de-anchor.
This is shown in Figure 8-7, which plots one-year and ten-year forecasts of inflation from a
survey of professional forecasters. As you can see, the one-year forecast increased, a lthough
nowhere close to the increase in actual inflation. The ten-year forecast moved very
little, reaching a peak of 2.4% in 2022, only slightly above the Fed’s target rate of 2%.
Thus, the amplification mechanism that led to very high inflation in the 1970s did not
operate and explains why, when commodity prices started turning around, so did inflation.
Why was this outcome so different from what happened in the 1970s? The likely
answer again: Because of increased credibility of central banks’ commitment to meeting
their target inflation, which led wage and price setters to assume that the high inflation
would decrease to go back to target over time.
At the time of writing, inflation is substantially lower than it was at its peak, but
still above the target inflation rate of 2%. The question facing the Fed is the following:
Assuming, as seems reasonable, that the commodity price shocks go away and shortages
are eventually resolved, will inflation go back to the target on its own, or does the Fed
have to slow the economy, allowing for some increase in unemployment?
Assume that expectations remain anchored so θ is equal to zero and πe = π. You can
see from equation (8.6) that this depends on whether the unemployment rate is equal to
or lower than the natural rate. There is wide agreement that, with unemployment below
4% at the time of writing, the labor market is overheating, that the unemployment rate is
below the natural unemployment rate, perhaps by 1%. If this assumption is correct, then
to get inflation down to target, the Fed will have to slow output growth and allow for some
increase in the unemployment rate.
By the time you read this book, you will likely know the outcome and be able to tell
the rest of the story.

8-4 A SUMMARY AND MANY WARNINGS
Let’s take stock of what we have learned:
■ The relation between inflation and unemployment depends on how wage setters
form expectations of inflation.
■ If expectations are anchored, as they were in the 1960s and have been again since
the mid-1990s, then the Phillips curve takes the form of a relation between inflation
and unemployment. Inflation is higher than expected if unemployment is below the
natural rate; it is lower than expected if unemployment is above the natural rate.
■ If, however, expectations are unanchored—as they became in the 1970s and
1980s—and expected inflation this year is equal to inflation last year, the Phillips
curve relation becomes a relation between the change in inflation and unem-
ployment. Inflation increases if unemployment is below the natural rate, and it
decreases if unemployment is above the natural rate.
■ The economy is sometimes hit by large supply shocks, such as an increase in the
price of oil or the shortages due to Covid. These shocks have a strong direct effect
on inflation. Whether the effect builds up and persists depends very much on how
strongly expectations are anchored.
In short, there is a relation between inflation and unemployment, but it is a complex
one. And, unfortunately, further warnings are in order: We saw in Chapter 7 that the
natural unemployment rate varies across countries, and we saw in the previous section
that it may vary over time. In this section, we discuss further two issues: the relation
between unemployment and inflation can simply disappear when inflation becomes very
high or when it becomes low and turns into deflation.
High Inflation and the Phillips Curve
Recall that, in the 1970s, the US Phillips curve changed as inflation became more per-
sistent and wage setters changed the way they formed inflation expectations. The lesson
is a general one. The relation between unemployment and inflation is likely to change
with the level and persistence of inflation. Evidence from countries with high inflation
confirms this. Not only does the way workers and firms form their expectations change,
but so do institutional arrangements.
When the inflation rate becomes high, inflation tends to become more variable. As More concretely, when infla-

Deflation and the Phillips Curve
We have just looked at what happens to the Phillips curve when inflation is high.
Another issue is what happens when inflation is low and possibly negative—that is,
when there is deflation.
One motivation for asking this question is given by an aspect of Figure 8-1 that
was mentioned at the start of the chapter. In that figure, the points corresponding to
the 1930s (denoted by triangles) lie to the right of the others. Not only was unemploy-
ment unusually high—this is no surprise because these years corresponded to the Great
Depression—but, given the high unemployment rate, the inflation rate was surprisingly
high. In other words, given the high unemployment rate, we would have expected not
merely deflation but a large rate of deflation. In fact, deflation was limited and, from
1934 to 1937, despite high unemployment, inflation actually turned positive.
How do we interpret this fact? There are two potential explanations.
If u n increases with u, then One is that the Great Depression was associated with an increase not only in the

an increase in the actual unemployment rate over the natural rate of unemployment,
rather than an increase in the natural rate of unemployment itself.
The other is that, when the economy experiences deflation, the Phillips curve rela-
tion breaks down. One possible reason is the reluctance of workers to accept decreases
in their nominal wages. Workers may unwittingly accept a cut in their real wages that
occurs when their nominal wages increase more slowly than inflation, but they are
likely to fight the same cut in their real wages if it results from an overt cut in their
nominal wages.
Figure 8-8, for example, plots the distribution of wage changes in Portugal in two
years: 1984, when the inflation rate was a high 27%, and 2012, when it was just 2.1%.
Note that the distribution of wage changes is roughly symmetric in 1984, but bunched
at zero in 2012, with nearly no negative wage changes. To the extent that this constraint
is at work, this implies that the Phillips curve relation between inflation and unem-
ployment may disappear, or at least become weaker, when the economy is close to zero
inflation.

176 The Medium Run The Core

From the Short to 9
the Medium Run:
The IS-LM-PC
Model
In Chapters 3 through 6 we looked at equilibrium in the goods and financial markets and
saw how, in the short run, output is determined by demand. In Chapters 7 and 8, we
looked at equilibrium in the labor market and derived how unemployment affects infla-
tion. We now put the two parts together to characterize the behavior of output, unem-
ployment, and inflation, in both the short run and the medium run. When confronted with a
macroeconomic question about a particular shock or a particular policy, this model, which
we shall call the IS-LM-PC (PC for Phillips curve), is typically the model I start from. I hope
you find it as useful as I do.
The chapter is organized as follows.
Section 9-1 develops the IS-LM-PC model.
Section 9-2 shows how the economy adjusts from its short-run equilibrium to its
medium-run equilibrium.
Section 9-3 discusses complications and how things can go wrong.
Section 9-4 looks at the dynamic effects of fiscal consolidation.
Section 9-5 concludes.
If you remember one basic message from this chapter, it should be: In the short
run, demand determines output. In the medium run, with the help of policy, output
returns to potential. ccc
177

--- PAGE 199 ---

9-1 THE IS-LM-PC MODEL
In Chapter 6, we looked at the implications of equilibrium in the goods and financial
markets.
From equilibrium in the goods market, we derived the following equation (equation 6.5)
for the behavior of output in the short run:
Y = C(Y −T)+ I(Y, r +x)+G (9.1)
In the short run, output is determined by demand. Demand is the sum of con-
sumption, investment, and government spending. Consumption depends on disposable
income, which is equal to income net of taxes. Investment depends on output and on
the real borrowing rate. The real borrowing rate is the sum of real rate, r, chosen by the
central bank, and a risk premium, x. Government spending is exogenous.
As we did in Chapter 6, we can draw the IS curve implied by equation (9.1), the
relation between output, Y, and the real rate, r, taking as given taxes T, the risk premium
x, and government spending G. This is done in the top half of Figure 9-1. The IS curve
is downward sloping: The lower the real rate, r, the higher the equilibrium level of out-
put. The mechanism behind the relation should be familiar by now: A lower real rate
increases investment. Higher investment leads to higher demand. Higher demand leads
to higher output. The increase in output further increases consumption and investment,
leading to a further increase in demand, and so on.
From equilibrium in financial markets, we derived a flat LM curve, showing the real
rate, r, chosen by the central bank. (We saw in Chapter 4 how, behind the scenes, the
central bank adjusts the supply of money so as to achieve its desired interest rate.)
IS
A
r LM
Y
Output
PC
A
p2p
Y Y
n
Output

--- PAGE 200 ---

It is drawn as the horizontal line at r in the top half of the figure. The intersection of the
downward sloping IS curve and the horizontal LM curve gives us the equilibrium level of
output in the short run.
From equilibrium in labor markets in Chapter 8, we derived the following equation
(equation 8.5) for the relation between inflation and unemployment, a relation we called
the Phillips curve (I am dropping the time indexes, which are not needed here):
π−πe = −α(u−u ) (9.2)
n
When the unemployment rate is lower than the natural rate, inflation is higher than
expected. If the unemployment rate is higher than the natural rate, inflation is lower
than expected.
Given that the first relation (equation (9.1)) is in terms of output, we must express
this Phillips curve relation in terms of output and inflation rather than unemployment
and inflation. It is easy, but it takes a few steps.
Start by looking at the relation between the unemployment rate and employment.
By definition, the unemployment rate is equal to unemployment divided by the labor
force: bFor a refresher, see Chapter 2.
u ≡ U L = (L−N) L = 1−N L
where N denotes employment and L denotes the labor force. The first equality is simply
the definition of the unemployment rate. The second equality follows from the defini-
tion of unemployment, and the third equality is obtained through simplification. The
unemployment rate is equal to one minus the ratio of employment to the labor force.
Reorganizing to express N as a function of u gives: bThis simplification has two
parts. First, that output is

given by N = L(1−u ) and output is equal to Y = L(1−u ). Call N the natural output, so we can set A to 1.

It follows that we can express the deviation of output from its natural level as:
Y −Y = L((1−u)−(1−u ))= −L(u−u )
n n n
This gives us a simple relation between the deviation of output from potential and
the deviation of unemployment from its natural rate. The difference between output and
potential output is called the output gap. If unemployment is equal to the natural rate,
output is equal to potential, and the output gap is equal to zero; if unemployment is
above the natural rate, output is below potential and the output gap is negative; and if
unemployment is below the natural rate, output is above potential and the output gap is
positive. (The relation of this equation to the actual relation between output and unem-
ployment, known as Okun’s law is explored further in the Focus Box “Okun’s Law across
Time and Countries.”)

Output Growth (percent)
We need to take one last step. We saw in Chapter 8 that the way wage setters form
expectations about inflation has changed through time. The evidence in Chapter 8 sug-
gests that, in the United States today, inflation expectations are anchored and a reason-
able assumption is that wage setters expect inflation to be equal to the target set by the
Fed, denoted π. For the moment, let’s make this assumption. I shall return to what happens
when it does not hold at various points in the chapter. With this assumption, the relation
between inflation and output is given by:
π−π = (α L)(Y −Y ) (9.4)
n
In words: When output is above potential and therefore the output gap is positive, Y >Y n ⇒ π >π
inflation exceeds target inflation. When output is below potential and therefore the out-bY <Y n ⇒ π <π
put gap is negative, inflation is lower than target inflation. This positive relation between Y =Y n ⇒ π = π
output and inflation is drawn as the upward sloping curve in the bottom half of Figure 9-1.
Output is measured on the horizontal axis, the change in inflation is measured on the
vertical axis. When output is equal to potential, equivalently when the output gap is
equal to zero, inflation is equal to target inflation. Thus, the Phillips curve crosses the
horizontal axis at the point where output is equal to potential.
We have derived two relations in this section. The first, derived from goods and fi-
nancial market equilibrium links the real interest rate to output. The second, derived from
labor market equilibrium, links output to inflation. With these two equations, we can
now describe what happens in the short and the medium run. This is what we do in the
next section.

--- PAGE 203 ---

9-2 FROM THE SHORT
TO THE MEDIUM RUN
Return to Figure 9-1. Assume that the real rate chosen by the central bank is equal to r.
The top part of the figure tells us that, associated with this interest rate, the level of out-
put is given by Y. The bottom part of the figure tells us that this level of output Y implies
an inflation rate equal to π. Given the way I have drawn the figure, Y is larger than Y ,
n
so output is above potential. There is a positive output gap, which implies that inflation
is higher than target inflation, π. Put less formally, the economy is overheating, putting
pressure on inflation. This is the short-run equilibrium.
What happens over time? Suppose the central bank leaves the real rate unchanged
at r. Then, output remains above potential and inflation remains above target. At some
stage, expectations are likely to adjust, and inflation expectations to deanchor. For the
moment, let’s assume that, to avoid such an outcome (we shall come back to it), the cen-
tral bank acts to reduce inflation back to target.
To do so, it must increase the interest rate, and the process of adjustment is described
in Figure 9-2. Let the initial equilibrium be denoted by point A in both the top and bot-
tom graphs. The central bank increases the real rate over time, shifting the LM curve up,
so the economy moves along the IS curve up from A to A′. Output decreases. Now turn to
PC curve is redundant
the bottom graph. As output decreases and the output gap shrinks, the economy moves
because the C already stands
for curve. But it will do.c down the PC curve from A to A′.
Figure 9-2

Inflation
Over the medium run, the
economy converges to poten-
tial output and inflation con-
r verges to target inflation. n

At point A′, the economy reaches its medium-run equilibrium. Let’s look at it more
closely.
In the medium run, output returns to its natural level: Y = Y . In parallel, unem-
n
ployment returns to the natural unemployment rate: u = u . With unemployment at
n
the natural rate, the inflation rate returns to the target rate π = π.
Turning to interest rates: The real rate must be such that the demand for goods is
equal to potential output, and is thus given by r = r in Figure 9-2. This interest rate is
n
often called the natural rate of interest (to reflect the fact that it is the rate of interest
associated with the natural rate of unemployment, or the natural level of output); it is
also sometimes called the neutral rate of interest or the Wicksellian rate of interest
(because the concept was introduced by Knut Wicksell, a Swedish economist who char-
acterized it at the end of the 19th century). The real borrowing rate is given in turn by
r +x, where x is the risk premium.
n
What about the nominal interest rate, i? Recall the relation between the nominal
and the real rate from Chapter 6 (equation 6.4): The real rate is equal to the nominal
rate minus expected inflation. Equivalently, the nominal rate is equal to the real rate plus
expected inflation: i = r + πe. Given that, in the medium run, the real rate is equal to
the neutral rate, and that expected inflation is equal to actual inflation, which is itself
equal to target inflation, this implies that the nominal rate is equal to i = r + π. The
n
higher the target inflation rate, the higher the nominal interest rate.
Finally, what about money and money growth? Recall the characterization of the
Recall that we are ignoring
equilibrium condition in Chapter 5 (equation 5.3) that the real supply of money equals boutput growth for the time
real money demand, M P = YL(i). Given that output is equal to potential, and the nom- being.
inal interest rate is determined from above, we can rewrite this equation as:
M
= Y L(r + π)
P n n
Note that all three variables on the right-hand side, natural output, the natural rate
of interest, and target inflation, are constant in steady state, and so the real demand for
money is constant in steady state. This implies that the left-hand side, the real supply
of money, must be constant. This in turn implies that the price level, P, must be growing
at the same rate as nominal money, M. Writing the rate of growth of money as g ,
M
this gives π = g . Replacing in the equation for the nominal interest rate, and using
M A summary of the medium-
the fact that π = π, we can rewrite the nominal rate as i = r + g . In the medium
n M run results: Y = Y , u = u ,
run, the nominal interest rate is equal to the real neutral rate plus the rate of nominal π = π = g , r = n r , n
M n
money growth. bi = r + g
n M
To summarize: In the medium run, the real variables, be it output, unemployment,
or the real rate of interest, are independent of monetary policy. What monetary policy
determines is the rate of inflation and the nominal interest rate. In the medium run, a
higher rate of money growth leads only to higher inflation and higher nominal interest
rates. The fact that monetary policy does not affect real variables in the medium run is
referred to as the neutrality of money.
9-3 COMPLICATIONS, AND HOW THINGS
CAN GO WRONG
The adjustment to the medium-run equilibrium in Section 9-2 seemed smooth and
easy. Indeed, as you were reading, you may have asked yourself why, if the central bank
wanted to return inflation to π and output to Y , didn’t it simply increase the real rate
n
from r to r right away, so that the medium-run equilibrium was reached without delay?
n
Chapter 9 From the Short to the Medium Run: The IS-LM-PC Model 183

--- PAGE 205 ---

The answer is that central banks would indeed like to keep the economy at Y = Y
n
and π = π but, although it looks easy to do in Figure 9-2, reality is more complicated.
Uncertainty
First, it is difficult for the central bank to know where potential output is exactly and thus
how far output is from potential. If inflation expectations are anchored, the deviation of
inflation from target provides a signal about the output gap (the distance between actual
and potential output), but in contrast to the simple equation (9.4), the signal is noisy.
Second, it takes time for the economy to respond. We discussed it in Chapter 5:
Adjustments do not take place instantaneously. Firms take time to adjust their investment
decisions. As investment spending slows down in response to the higher real rate, leading
to lower demand, lower output, and lower income, it takes time for consumers to adjust to
the decrease in income and for firms to adjust to the decrease in sales. In short, even if the
central bank acts quickly, it takes time for the economy to go back to potential output.
At the time of writing, the Fed is indeed facing both issues.
On the first: The unemployment rate, in December 2022, is equal to 3.5%. While it
is generally believed that this is lower than the natural rate, the issue is how much lower,
and thus what unemployment rate should the Fed aim for?
On the second: The Fed started increasing the federal funds rate in February 2022, when
the unemployment rate was 3.8%. At the time of writing, ten months later, the unemploy-
ment rate is actually even lower, at 3.5%. The contractionary monetary policy does not
appear to have had an effect yet. Is it coming? If so, it is reasonable to wait. Or is monetary
policy not contractionary enough, and the federal funds rate must be increased further?
None of these questions has a clear-cut answer, making the job of the Fed particularly difficult.
Deanchoring
One reasonable reaction to the discussion above is that, if the central bank is uncertain
about the value of the natural unemployment rate, and by implication, about the level
of potential output, it should proceed slowly, and get a better sense from the behavior of
unemployment and inflation along the way.
Or should it? Suppose that the central bank indeed moves slowly, so inflation remains
higher than target inflation, for some time. It is likely that, at some point, wage setters
will change the way they form expectations and start assuming that inflation will keep
exceeding the target. Put another way, inflation expectations may deanchor.
We explored this possibility in Chapter 8. We assumed that expected inflation was
given by πe = (1−θ)π +θπ , and we defined deanchoring as an increase in θ from
t t−1
0 possibly all the way to 1. To see what this implies for the issue at hand, and to keep the
algebra simple, assume that inflation lasts long enough that θ increases from 0 all the
way to 1. (The conclusions are qualitatively the same when θ becomes positive, although
remaining less than 1). Figure 9-3 shows what happens.
Figure 9-3 resembles closely Figure 9-2, but with one major difference. As
πe = π(−1), the equation represented by the bottom graph is given by:
π−π(−1)= (α L)(Y −Y )
n
Thus, the variable on the vertical axis in the bottom graph is the change in the infla-
tion rather than the deviation of inflation from a fixed target as in Figure 9-2. This has an
important implication: So long as output exceeds potential output, inflation increases.
So, when output is back to potential, inflation, which has increased until then, is higher
than the target. If the central bank wants to get back to the target, it must engineer a
period of output below potential—equivalently a period of unemployment above the
natural rate—a painful outcome.
184 The Medium Run The Core

--- PAGE 206 ---

Figure 9-3
Implications
of Deanchored
Expectations.
If expectations fully deanchor,
inflation keeps increasing
r n as long as inflation exceeds
target inflation. Thus, when
r unemployment is back to the
natural rate, inflation will be
substantially higher than the
target.
Output
So, let’s go back to what the central bank should do. On the one hand, uncertainty
suggests going slowly. On the other hand, the risk of deanchoring increases with the
length of time during which inflation exceeds the central bank target. This suggests
going fast, so that inflation returns quickly to target and expectations do not have time
to deanchor. This leads to two conclusions. First, the higher the initial credibility of the
target and the commitment of the central bank to achieve it, the less likely is deanchor-
ing and thus the easier the job of the central bank. This explains why central banks care
very much about credibility. Second, even if they are credible, finding the narrow way
between going too slow or going too fast remains a major challenge for monetary policy.
I have focused on an economy where demand led to output above potential. Very
much the same logic applies to supply shocks, such as an increase in the price of energy.
We saw in Chapter 8 that we could think of such an increase as an increase in the
markup of prices over wages, an increase in m, and by implication, an increase in the natural
unemployment rate and an associated decrease in potential output. Thus, if the econ-
omy starts at potential output, and if output remains the same but potential output
decreases, this creates a positive output gap, which leads to more inflation. The outcome
looks very much like Figure 9-2, except for the fact that the output gap comes from a
decrease in potential output rather than an increase in output.
What should the central bank then do? Suppose it believes that (1) the shock will
not last—so m will return quickly to its previous value—(2) that workers will accept
the implied real wage decline—that a decrease in z will largely offset the increase in m,
leading to little change in (m+ z) and thus little change in the natural unemployment

rate and in potential output—and (3) that expectations will not deanchor—that θ will
remain equal to zero. Then, it may decide not to tighten, keep output at the same level,
and accept the higher inflation so long as the shock lasts. When the shock disappears,
inflation will naturally return to target.
If any of the three assumptions fail however, such a strategy may prove costly. If
shocks last longer than initially forecast, inflation will last longer. If workers do not accept
a decrease in the real wage, inflation will be stronger. And if inflation is longer and stron-
ger, expectations may deanchor. Then, the scenario is similar to the scenario we described
in Figure 9-2. Inflation may steadily increase, and even when the shocks are gone, will be
higher than target inflation. To get it back to target, the central bank will have to engineer a
period of painful unemployment, with output below potential for some period.
If I had to convince you of the difficulty of managing monetary policy, as well as
the importance of monetary policy credibility, this discussion should do it. The Focus
box above, “Oil Price Increases: Why Were the Effects in the 2000s Different from the
1970s?” provides a clear example of the importance of credibility.
The next subsection explores a very different danger, which arises when the central
bank is facing the zero lower bound.

to even larger deflation, interest rate, the lower output, and so on, a situation known as a deflation spiral or a
and so on. deflation trap. As indicated by the two arrows in the figure, instead of returning to its
medium-run equilibrium, the economy moves further away, with lower and lower out-
put and larger and large deflation.
This nightmare scenario is not just a theoretical concern. It played out during the
Great Depression. As shown in the Focus Box “Deflation in the Great Depression,” from
1929 to 1933 inflation turned into larger and larger deflation, steadily increasing the
real rate and decreasing spending and output, until other policy measures were taken,
and the economy started turning around.
More recently, a similar worry arose during the Global Financial Crisis. With the
nominal rate down to zero in the major advanced countries, the worry was that inflation
would turn negative and start a similar spiral. Thankfully, it did not happen. Inflation
decreased and, in some countries—Greece, Spain, and Portugal, for example—turned
to deflation. This limited the ability of the central bank to decrease the real rate and
increase output, but deflation remained limited, and the feared deflation spiral did not
happen. The reason for this, which connects to our previous discussion of expectation
formation, is that inflation expectations remained largely anchored. Low output led to
low inflation and in some cases mild deflation, but not to steadily larger deflation, as was
the case during the Great Depression.

discussed in Chapter 5 and look at not only its short-run effects but its medium-run
effects as well.

the left, from IS to IS′. Assume, for the moment, that the central bank keeps the same real

Fiscal Consolidation
in the Short and the
Medium Run
Fiscal consolidation leads to
a decrease in output in the
short run. In the medium run,
output returns to potential,
and the interest rate is lower.
start with, the fiscal consolidation, as desirable as it may be on other grounds, leads to a
recession. This is the short-run equilibrium we characterized in Section 5-3 of Chapter 5.
Note that, as income comes down and taxes increase, consumption decreases on both
counts. Note also that, as output decreases, so does investment. In the short run, on
macroeconomic grounds, fiscal consolidation looks rather unappealing: It triggers a
recession, and both consumption and investment go down.
Let’s, however, turn to the dynamics and to the medium run. As output is too low,
and inflation is below target, the central bank is likely to react and decrease the policy
rate until output is back to potential. In terms of Figure 9-5, the LM curve shifts down,
and the economy moves down the IS′ curve in the top graph and output increases. As
output increases, the economy moves up the PC curve in the bottom graph until output
is back to potential. Thus, the medium-run equilibrium is given by point A′′ in both the
top and bottom graphs. Output is back at Y and inflation is again equal to target infla-
n
tion. The real rate needed to maintain output at potential is now lower than before, equal
to r′ rather than r .
n n
Now look at the composition of output in this new equilibrium. As income is the
same as it was before the fiscal consolidation but taxes are higher, consumption is lower,
although not as low as it was in the short run. As output is the same as before but the
interest rate is lower, investment is higher than before. In other words, the decrease in
consumption is offset by an increase in investment, so demand, and by implication out-
put, is unchanged. This is in sharp contrast to what happened in the short run and makes
fiscal consolidation look more attractive. Although consolidation decreases investment in
the short run, it increases investment in the medium run.
This discussion raises some of the same issues we discussed in the previous section.

It looks as if fiscal consolidation could take place without a decrease in output in the
short run. All that is needed is for the central bank and the government to coordinate
carefully. As fiscal consolidation takes place, the central bank should decrease the real
rate so as to maintain output at the natural level. In other words, the proper combina-
tion of fiscal and monetary policy could achieve the medium-run equilibrium outcome
in the short run. Such coordination indeed happens sometimes; as we saw in Chapter 5,
it happened in the United States in the 1990s, when a fiscal consolidation was accom-
panied with a monetary expansion. But it does not always happen. One reason is that
the central bank may be unable to decrease the real rate sufficiently because of the zero This has led to an intense

9-5 CONCLUSIONS
This chapter has covered a lot of ground. Let me repeat some key ideas and develop some
of the conclusions.
The Short Run versus the Medium Run
One key message of this chapter is that shocks or changes in policy typically have different
effects in the short run and in the medium run. Disagreements among economists about
the effects of various policies often come from differences in the time frame they have in
mind. If you are worried about output and investment in the short run, you might be reluc-
tant to proceed with fiscal consolidation. But if your focus is on the medium and long run,
you will see the consolidation as helping investment and eventually, through higher invest-
ment and thus capital accumulation, increasing output. One implication is that where you
stand depends, among other things, on how fast you think the economy adjusts to shocks.
If you believe that it takes a long time for output to return to potential you will naturally
focus more on the short run and be willing to use policies that increase output in the short
run, even if medium-run effects are nil or negative. If you believe instead that output re-
How to define shocks is
turns to potential quickly, you will put more emphasis on the medium-run implications harder than it looks. Suppose
and will, by implication, be more reluctant to use those policies. a failed economic program
in a Latin American country
Shocks and Propagation Mechanisms leads to political chaos in
that country, which leads to

region or even more broadly,
times called business cycles)—movements in output around its trend (a trend that we
which leads to a fall in
have ignored so far but on which we will focus in Chapters 10 through 13).
consumer confidence in the
You can think of the economy as being constantly hit by shocks. These shocks United States, which leads

Each shock has dynamic effects on output and its components. These dynamic effects dence? In practice, we have
are called the propagation mechanism of the shock. Propagation mechanisms are to cut the chain of causation

Chapter 9 From the Short to the Medium Run: The IS-LM-PC Model 191

--- PAGE 213 ---

combinations that they create a recession. The two US recessions of the 1970s were due
largely to increases in the price of oil; a recession in the early 1980s was due to a sharp
tightening of monetary policy; a recession in the early 1990s was due primarily to a sud-
den decline in consumer confidence; the recession of 2001 was due to a sharp drop in
investment spending. The sharp decrease in output in 2009 had its origins in the prob-
lems of the housing market, which led to a major financial shock and in turn to a sharp
reduction in output. The decrease in output in 2020 was due to the Covid shock and the
lockdowns. What we call economic fluctuations are the result of these shocks and their
dynamic effects on output.
Typically, the economy returns over time to its medium-run equilibrium. A major
issue is whether one can expect the economy to return to that equilibrium on its own,
or whether it requires an active policy response. While this is a controversial issue, I
believe that the economy does not self-stabilize, and that an active policy response,
whether through monetary or through fiscal policy, is typically needed. Indeed, in all the
examples we saw in this chapter, a reaction from the central bank was the mechanism
through which output returned to potential output over time. In the absence of a policy
reaction, inflation would have either steadily increased or decreased, eventually requir-
ing a more painful policy adjustment. This was particularly clear in the discussion of the
zero lower bound case: To the extent that monetary policy could no longer be used, there
was a risk that the economy may enter a deflation spiral, getting steadily worse over
time. During the Global Financial Crisis, the worst was avoided, partly because the finan-
cial system slowly recovered and partly because of the use of fiscal policy.

The Long Run
The next four chapters focus on the long run. In the long run,
what dominates is not fluctuations, but growth. So now we
need to ask: What determines growth?
197
01
retpahC
Chapter 10 looks at the facts of growth. It first documents the large increase in output
that has taken place in rich countries since 1950. Then, taking a wider look, it shows
that on the scale of human history, such growth is a recent phenomenon. And it is not
a universal phenomenon: Some countries are catching up, but some poor countries
are suffering from no or low growth.
11
retpahC
Chapter 11 focuses on the role of capital accumulation in growth. It shows that capital
accumulation cannot by itself sustain growth, but that it does affect the level of output.
A higher saving rate typically leads to lower consumption initially, but to more con-
sumption in the long run.
21
retpahC Chapter 12 turns to technological progress. It shows how, in the long run, the growth rate
of an economy is determined by the rate of technological progress. It then looks at the role
of research and development, and institutions in generating such progress.
31
retpahC
EROC
EHT
Chapter 13 looks at some of the challenges to growth. It discusses whether we can expect
technological progress to continue at the same rate, whether AI and robots might lead to
mass unemployment, whether growth necessarily comes with rising inequality and what can
be done about it, and whether we can mitigate climate change without giving up on growth.
By the end of Chapter 13, you will have seen the three conceptual models most macroeconomists use to ana-
lyze the short run (the IS-M model), the medium run (The IS-LM-PC model), and the long run (the Solow model).
Together, these provide the foundations you need to understand the basic macroeconomic mechanisms.

Figure 10-1 shows the evolution of US GDP (panel (a)) and of US GDP per person
(panel (b)) (both in 2012 dollars) since 1890. (The scale used to measure GDP or GDP per

The vertical bars from 1929 to 1933 correspond to the large decrease in output during
the Great Depression: the other sets of bars correspond to the 1980–1982 recession, which
was the largest postwar recession until the financial crisis, to the Global Financial Crisis of
2008–2010, and to the Covid recession of 2020. Note how small these four episodes appear
compared to the steady increase in output per person over the last 130 years. The cartoon
makes the same point about growth and fluctuations, in an even more obvious way.

Section 10-1 discusses a central measurement issue; namely, how to measure the stan­

Section 10-2 looks at growth in the United States and other rich countries over the last
50 years.
Section 10-3 takes a broader look, across both time and space.
Section 10-4 gives a primer on growth and introduces the framework that will be devel­
oped in the next three chapters.
If you remember one basic message from this chapter, it should be: Over long peri­
ods of time, growth dwarfs fluctuations. The (complex) key to sustained growth is
technological progress.

10-1 MEASURING THE STANDARD
OF LIVING
The fundamental reason we care about the growth of a country is that we care about
the average standard of living in the country. Looking across time, we want to know
by how much the standard of living has increased. Looking across countries, we want to
know how much higher the standard of living is in one country relative to another. Thus,
the variable we want to focus on, and compare either over time or across countries, is
output per person, rather than output itself.
A practical problem then arises: How do we compare output per person across coun-
tries? Countries use different currencies; thus, output in each country is expressed in terms
of its own currency. A natural solution would seem to use exchange rates. When comparing,
